metadata.title: Homeostasis During Fasting. II. Hormone Substrate Differences Between Men and Women
metadata.journal: Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism
metadata.study_design: Comparative experimental study with fasting protocol
metadata.demographics: Ten normal men and 9 normal women, premenopausal

evidence.quote: Ten normal men and 9 normal women were fasted for 72 hr.
Four men, matching 4 of the women in weight and age, were also fasted a second time while receiving ethinylestradiol (0.1 mg twice daily).
Premenopausal women had significantly lower concentrations of glucose during fasting than men.
The mean lowest glucose concentration in plasma in the male group during the 3 days of starvation was 66.4 ± 2.9 mg/100 ml. The corresponding figure in females was 47.8 ±2.9 mg/100 ml (p < 0.01).
(/ Clin Endocrinol Metab 37: 698, 1973)
Ten normal men and 9 normal women were fasted for 72 hr.
Ten normal men and 9 normal women were fasted for 72 hr.
Ten normal men and 9 normal women were fasted for 72 hr.
Four men, matching 4 of the women in weight and age, were also fasted a second time while receiving ethinylestradiol (0.1 mg twice daily).
Four men, matching 4 of the women in weight and age, were also fasted a second time while receiving ethinylestradiol (0.1 mg twice daily).
Five of the women were fasted in both the preovulatory and postovulatory phases of the menstrual cycle.
Prior to admission, all subjects ingested a diet containing a minimum of 150 g of carbyhodrate and 1 g/kg of body weight of protein for at least 2 weeks.
Prior to admission, all subjects ingested a diet containing a minimum of 150 g of carbyhodrate and 1 g/kg of body weight of protein for at least 2 weeks.
Prior to admission, all subjects ingested a diet containing a minimum of 150 g of carbyhodrate and 1 g/kg of body weight of protein for at least 2 weeks.
Each 3-day fast was preceded by a 2-day control period.
Samples were obtained at 8 AM and 8 PM daily.
Glucose was measured in triplicate by a glucose oxidase method (4).

methods_participants.label: Glucose Assay - Coefficient of Variation Within Assay

evidence.quote: The coefficient of variation between triplicates in a given assay was 1.5% and between assays, 2.5%.

methods_participants.label: Glucose Assay - Coefficient of Variation Between Assays

evidence.quote: The coefficient of variation between triplicates in a given assay was 1.5% and between assays, 2.5%.
FFA were measured in duplicate by the method of Trout, Estes and Friedberg using palmitic acid standards and a 4 point standard curve (5).

methods_participants.label: Insulin Assay Method
methods_participants.value: modifications of the methods of Berson and Yalow
methods_participants.method: modifications of the methods of Berson and Yalow

evidence.quote: Serum immunoreactive insulin, HGH and glucagon were assayed by modifications of the methods of Berson and Yalow, and Roth, Click, Yalow and Berson, respectively (6,7).
Glucagon was measured using a charcoal separatory method and antiserum for pancreatic glucagon (30 K) which was obtained from Dr. Roger Unger.

methods_participants.label: HGH Assay - Duplicate Variation (0-5 range)
methods_participants.method: modifications of the methods of Roth, Click, Yalow and Berson

evidence.quote: HGH samples were checked in duplicate with the maximum variation between duplicates being 0.5 ng/ml in the 0 to 5 range, and 1.0 ng/ml in the 5 to 15 range.

methods_participants.label: HGH Assay - Duplicate Variation (5-15 range)
methods_participants.method: modifications of the methods of Roth, Click, Yalow and Berson

evidence.quote: HGH samples were checked in duplicate with the maximum variation between duplicates being 0.5 ng/ml in the 0 to 5 range, and 1.0 ng/ml in the 5 to 15 range.

methods_participants.label: Insulin Assay - Replicates
methods_participants.method: modifications of the methods of Berson and Yalow

evidence.quote: To obtain maximum accuracy within the range of normal basal values of insulin, samples were run in quadruplicate, the final reported value being the mean of 4 determinations.

methods_participants.label: Insulin Assay - Maximum Variation in Basal Range
methods_participants.method: modifications of the methods of Berson and Yalow

evidence.quote: Maximum variation in the basal range (0 to 15) was 1.0 u.U/ml.
For glucagon measurements, samples were run in triplicate with appropriate control tubes for each sample.
No subject had CHO intolerance and hepatic screening tests were normal.
No subject had CHO intolerance and hepatic screening tests were normal.

methods_participants.label: Inclusion Criteria - Medication
methods_participants.value: No subject was receiving any type of medication

evidence.quote: No srarjject was receiving any type of medication.

methods_participants.label: Menstrual Cycle Timing - Preovulatory
methods_participants.value: 4 to 5 days after the last day of menstrual bleeding

evidence.quote: The former study was conducted 4 to 5 days after the last day of menstrual bleeding; the latter, 15 to 17 days after the last day of menstrual bleeding.

methods_participants.label: Menstrual Cycle Timing - Postovulatory
methods_participants.value: 15 to 17 days after the last day of menstrual bleeding

evidence.quote: The former study was conducted 4 to 5 days after the last day of menstrual bleeding; the latter, 15 to 17 days after the last day of menstrual bleeding.

narrative_results.statement: With prolonged fasting, plasma glucose and insulin concentrations decreased in both men and women, reaching a plateau on the second or third day of the fast.

evidence.quote: With prolonged fasting, plasma glucose and insulin concentrations decreased in both men and women, reaching a plateau on the second or third day of the fast.

narrative_results.statement: There was a striking sex-based difference in the pattern of glucose change, with women showing lower plasma glucose levels than men during fasting.

evidence.quote: There was a striking sex-based difference in the pattern of glucose change. In women, plasma glucose decreased to 52.5 ±3.4 mg/100 ml at 60 hr vs 67.5 ±2.0 mg/100 ml in men.

narrative_results.statement: The difference between men and women while fasting was significant at the 1% level.

evidence.quote: The difference between men and women while fasting was significant at the 1% level.

narrative_results.statement: At 36, 48 and 60 hr of the fast, plasma glucagon concentrations were significantly higher in women than in men (p < 0.01).

evidence.quote: At 36, 48 and 60 hr of the fast, plasma glucagon concentrations were significantly higher in women than in men (p < 0.01).

narrative_results.statement: Changes of plasma glucose and glucagon were similar in each study period when women were fasted in both the preovulatory and postovulatory phase.

evidence.quote: Changes of plasma glucose and glucagon were similar in each study period

narrative_results.statement: Men treated with ethinylestradiol did not exhibit a metabolic pattern comparable to women matched in age and weight.

evidence.quote: men treated with ethinylestradiol did not exhibit a metabolic pattern comparable to women matched in age and weight.

narrative_results.statement: There is a greater decrease of plasma glucose in women than in men during fasting.

evidence.quote: there is a greater decrease of plasma glucose in women than in men during fasting

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass 1 reports 4 men and 4 women; Pass 2 reports only 4 men, omitting women count
Pass_1 discusses glucose differences between sexes while fasting; pass_2 discusses glucagon concentrations. These are different metabolites and findings
Pass_1 is about glucagon concentrations; pass_2 is about glucose decrease. Different metabolites and findings despite both mentioning sex differences

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Four men, matching 4 of the women in weight and age, were also fasted a second time while receiving ethinylestradiol (0.1 mg twice daily).
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Four men, matching 4 of the women in weight and age, were also fasted a second time while receiving ethinylestradiol

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Menstrual Cycle Timing - Postovulatory
There was a striking sex-based difference in the pattern of glucose change. In women, plasma glucose decreased to 52.5 ±3.4 mg/100 ml at 60 hr vs 67.5 ±2.0 mg/100 ml in men.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: There was a striking sex-based difference in the pattern of glucose change.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: There was a striking sex-based difference in the pattern of glucose change, with women showing lower plasma glucose levels than men during fasting.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: There was a striking sex-based difference in the pattern of glucose change.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: The difference between men and women while fasting was significant at the 1% level.

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Changes of plasma glucose and glucagon were similar in each study period (Fig. 2, Table 1).

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Changes of plasma glucose and glucagon were similar in each study period when women were fasted in both the preovulatory and postovulatory phase.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Changes of plasma glucose and glucagon were similar in each study period in preovulatory and postovulatory phases.

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: There is a greater decrease of plasma glucose in women than in men during fasting. [sex difference]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: There was a striking sex-based difference in the pattern of glucose change. [sex difference]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: With prolonged fasting, plasma glucose and insulin concentrations decreased in both men and women, reaching a plateau on the second or third day of the fast.
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: With prolonged fasting, plasma glucose and insulin concentrations decreased in both men and women, reaching a plateau on the second or third day of the fast.

semantic_groups.rationale: Identical study population description
Identical fasting duration specification
Identical dosing information
Pass 1 reports 4 men and 4 women; Pass 2 reports only 4 men, omitting women count
Same participant count and gender; different phrasing of study context
Identical carbohydrate requirement specification
Identical protein requirement specification
Identical pre-fasting diet duration specification
Same fact: 2-day control period before fasting. Only difference is label wording ('Before Fasting' vs 'Duration').
Identical extraction: blood sampling at 8 AM and 8 PM daily.
Same assay method (glucose oxidase). Pass_2 truncates 'method' but the fact is identical.
Same within-assay CV value (1.5%). Minor wording difference in label and pass_2 truncates 'method'.
Same between-assay CV value (2.5%). Minor wording difference in label and pass_2 truncates 'method'.
Same FFA assay method. Pass_2 truncates 'method' but the fact is identical.
Same insulin assay method. Pass_2 omits 'modifications of the methods of' prefix, losing important context.
Same glucagon assay method (charcoal separatory). Pass_2 truncates 'method' but the fact is identical.

Same assay and values; pass_1 lists four method sources (Roth, Click, Yalow, Berson) while pass_2 mentions only Berson and Yalow. This is a difference in attribution detail but describes the same underlying method modification.
Same assay and values; pass_1 lists four method sources while pass_2 mentions only Berson and Yalow. Consistent with pair 16 - difference in attribution detail.
Same assay, replicates, and method modification. Wording differs only in 'modifications of the methods of' vs 'modification' but conveys identical information.

Same assay and value (1.0); pass_1 uses 'u.U/ml' while pass_2 uses 'μU/ml' (equivalent units); pass_1 says 'Maximum Variation in Basal Range' while pass_2 says 'Basal Range Variation' - minor phrasing difference.
Same assay and replicates; pass_1 says 'charcoal separatory method' while pass_2 says 'charcoal separatory' - negligible wording difference.
Identical text in both passes.
Identical text in both passes.
Identical text in both passes.
Both describe the same preovulatory timing (4-5 days after menstrual bleeding ends). Only difference is 'Menstrual Cycle Timing' vs 'Menstrual Cycle Phase - Preovulatory Timing' in labeling.
Both describe the same postovulatory timing (15-17 days after menstrual bleeding ends). Only difference is 'Menstrual Cycle Timing' vs 'Menstrual Cycle Phase - Postovulatory Timing' in labeling.
Identical extraction: 10 normal men in study population.
Both describe the same finding: women have lower plasma glucose than men during fasting, with pass_2 providing specific numerical values
Pass_1 discusses glucose differences between sexes while fasting; pass_2 discusses glucagon concentrations. These are different metabolites and findings
Pass_1 is about glucagon concentrations; pass_2 is about glucose decrease. Different metabolites and findings despite both mentioning sex differences
Both convey the same finding about similar changes in glucose and glucagon across menstrual phases; pass_1 explicitly mentions women were fasted while pass_2 is implicit
Identical statements about ethinylestradiol-treated men not matching women's metabolic pattern
Both describe sex-based differences in glucose during fasting; pass_1 is more specific about the direction and magnitude of change
Identical statements about glucose and insulin decreases during prolonged fasting in both sexes
